data.title: Tariff risks weigh on US growth, but India remains a high-conviction market: HSBC’s Xavier Baraton
data.description:
Global uncertainty and tariff risks continue to pressure the US economy, with HSBC Asset Management expecting growth of around 1.5% and further rate cuts from the Federal Reserve.

Yet, despite headwinds, Global CIO Xavier Baraton maintains a pro-risk but selective investment stance—identifying opportunities in emerging markets and reaffirming India’s long-term appeal as valuations turn more reasonable.

Below are the edited excerpts from the interview.

Q: There's so much happening globally. We are talking about tariffs, we are talking about inflation in some economies, and we are talking about growth possibly slowing down somewhere. What's your sense? How are we specifically looking at the US markets? Are the tariffs benefiting that economy?

A: At the moment, the tariff risk and global uncertainty have generally been weighing on the US economy. We anticipate around 1.5% economic growth, which is actually much lower than what we've experienced in the US economy over the past five years. By contrast—and it's a bit of an inverted image—inflation is persistent and sticky, and it poses a particular challenge for the Fed, which is nonetheless on track to deliver a few more rate cuts after starting the easing cycle in September.

So, we expect two more cuts by the end of the year and another two or three next year already. From an investment standpoint—and accepting that we need to navigate these choppy seas with a lot of care—we still think it’s a pro-risk environment, generally speaking, but we're very targeted and selective in our allocations.

Typically, we are looking at stock markets where we see attractive valuations. In particular, there are lots of pockets that are starting to look a little lofty from a valuation standpoint. I mean, we are probably talking about artificial intelligence (AI) and tech, I am sure. Emerging markets are very interesting and competitive, we think, and it comes at a moment when investors are really starting to shift after more than a decade of US exceptionalism. So that’s really how we are positioned at the moment.

Q: India has been a high-conviction market for HSBC. Now that you have been in India, what has been the key takeaway, because the foreign institutional investors (FIIs) so far are still shunning India?

A: That’s true, and I think it’s been a mixed experience for a lot of investors over the past year. But for us, we have long been overweight India in our portfolios, and for us, the story is still very much valid from both a cyclical and structural perspective.

It’s a combination of a stable economic environment and a stable government. We now have the Goods and Services Tax (GST) reforms and the Real Estate Regulatory Authority (RERA). Of course, the tariff uncertainty has cast some doubts and probably led investors to shy away—maybe pivoting, as some of them have, to China, as we were seeing signs of improvement there.

However, the structural factors are still very much present. From a valuation perspective, valuations have adjusted, which is very good, because India was often perceived as a pretty pricey market. But now, if we look at the traditional indices, we are back to the long-term average versus other emerging countries.

You are absolutely right that the FIIs have been more cautious; however, we are technically at the lower end in terms of underweighting India. We expect that risks are well-priced at this juncture, and we have reintroduced an overweight on Indian stocks.

Fixed income is interesting as well—we should never forget the opportunities in bond markets. The 10-year yield at the moment, at around 6.5%, really makes sense. The currency has depreciated, but in fact, again, it’s a mechanical effect of tariff uncertainty, which is probably at the worst possible scenario at the moment in terms of pricing. So again, possibly an opportunity.

Q: You mentioned China and South Korea, the two outperforming markets this year. What would it take now for FIIs to come back to India, considering those markets have seen a significant run-up, but India has not?

A: They probably want to see some sort of triggers on the tariff discussions. But generally, investors need to take a long-term stance on India and take the opportunity of adjusted valuations to return, really, because stock markets will always trade at some sort of premium.

We think, generally speaking, the structural improvement means that stocks will definitely re-rate. We tend to contrast China and India in terms of allocation. From our perspective, especially with what’s happening in terms of uncertainty or fluctuating correlations in developed markets, investors must be looking for diversifiers—very much so.

The US dollar is no longer necessarily perceived as a safe-haven asset. And if I take the example of local rates, they have been less volatile this year than rates in developed markets. It’s quite a life-changing environment. So, allocating more to emerging countries—both China and India—for us is relevant. It’s been a story that has been working well with our clients and investors, but it’s only now that we are starting to see flows really coming in.

Q: In India, which themes that you think will work for you? What are the focus points for HSBC?

A: We are very much weighted toward long-term themes—digitalisation, consumption, infrastructure, and financialisation as well. We see the wealth market expanding extremely fast. We are probably a bit more cautious at the moment on exporters, because you are never too sure exactly who and how they are going to be impacted, and which industry.

But organic growth is the common theme for the country, and we are very confident the country will simply apply the same strategies that have been so successful for India generally over the past decade, being the trigger for investors to return.

data.title: Arch Capital: Specialty Insurance Discipline at a Global Scale
data.description:
Introduction

Arch Capital Group isn't a household name like Chubb or Travelers, but within specialty insurance and reinsurance, it has built a reputation for delivering consistently high returns on equity through discipline and diversification. Founded in 1995 and headquartered in Bermuda, Arch has grown into a $ 30 billion+ market capitalization insurer with operations spanning property, casualty, mortgage, and reinsurance. Unlike many peers that focus narrowly on one line, Arch's diversified book gives it resilience across cycles while maintaining underwriting precision. What sets Arch apart is its ability to generate high teens ROE without relying on excessive leverage or risky assets. Over the last decade, book value per share has compounded at more than 13% annually, placing Arch among the fastest-growing large insurers. Even after this record, the market continues to price Arch at At roughly 1.9 book value (fluctuating between 1.7 and 2.0 in recent years), Arch trades at a premium to most property and casualty peer, a reflection of its underwriting consistency and disciplined growth model.

Insurance is rarely glamorous, and Arch doesn't chase headlines. Instead, it builds long-term shareholder value the same way it writes policies: cautiously, with an eye toward sustainable profitability. That model has quietly produced returns that rival more celebrated names, but with less attention from investors. For those willing to look past the lack of fanfare, Arch offers the traits value-focused investors prize: durable economics, prudent management, and a record of disciplined growth.

Business Model

Arch Capital's strength lies in its three-pillar model: insurance, reinsurance, and mortgage. Together, they form a diversified engine that balances cyclical risk while compounding book value steadily over time. Insurance is Arch's largest segment, covering property, casualty, professional liability, and specialty risks. Here, Arch competes in niches that demand underwriting expertise, from excess casualty to specialty lines like cyber and marine. By focusing on higher-barrier markets, the company maintains underwriting margins above peers. In 2023, the insurance segment generated over half of Arch's gross premiums written, with combined ratios averaging in the low 90s, consistently outperforming the industry.

Reinsurance provides a counterbalance. While catastrophe exposure can be volatile, Arch has deliberately kept a leaner cat book relative to global reinsurers like Swiss Re or Munich Re. Instead, it focuses on specialty proportional treaties and casualty reinsurance, which carry lower volatility and allow Arch to compound capital more steadily. This mix helped Arch weather years like 2017-2018, when major hurricanes strained global reinsurers, with far less downside. Mortgage insurance, Arch's third leg, adds stability through housing cycles. Following its acquisition of United Guaranty in 2016, Arch became the largest mortgage insurer in the U.S., giving it a recurring, fee-like revenue stream tied to home lending. Mortgage insurance typically produces combined ratios below 70, making it one of the most profitable segments in the group. While housing downturns pose risks, Arch has managed its credit exposure conservatively, with risk-to-capital ratios consistently below peers.The interplay of these three segments is Arch's edge. Insurance provides consistent profits, mortgage adds high-margin stability, and reinsurance offers optional upside in harder markets. Together, they allow Arch to deliver returns on equity that routinely exceed 15%, a level many peers cannot sustain. This diversified architecture is why Arch commands a modest premium multiple compared to other insurers, not because the market is overpaying, but because the model is designed to be more resilient.

This disciplined, decentralized underwriting framework doesn't just shape how Arch earns, it also defines how it deploys capital. The company's conservative reserving and risk-aware structure allow management to reinvest only when returns meet strict thresholds, reinforcing a culture of measured, compounding growth.

Capital Allocation

Arch Capital's strength doesn't just lie in underwriting discipline; it's equally defined by how management deploys capital. Unlike many insurers that lean heavily on dividends to placate investors, Arch prefers to reinvest and repurchase with surgical precision. This approach has amplified book value growth without sacrificing flexibility. Over the past decade, Arch has compounded book value per share at a double-digit rate, aided by disciplined share buybacks executed during periods of market stress. In 2020, for example, management stepped in aggressively when the stock dipped below tangible book value, turning volatility into long-term per-share accretion. Repurchases have totaled more than $3 billion since 2015, shrinking the share count by nearly 20%.

Dividends, by contrast, remain modest, a signal of prioritizing retained earnings for higher-return uses. The company's conservative reserving and strong capital buffers (with a solvency ratio comfortably above peer averages) give it optionality to lean into repurchases when dislocations arise. The model resembles Berkshire Hathaway's preference for opportunistic buybacks rather than rigid payout formulas. Just as importantly, Arch's balance sheet discipline ensures flexibility for growth. The company has avoided the trap of overpaying for acquisitions, instead focusing on organic underwriting growth and niche bolt-ons in mortgage and reinsurance that expand scale without stretching returns. That combination of opportunistic repurchases, measured dividends, and selective reinvestment forms a capital allocation flywheel that keeps intrinsic value compounding at a faster pace than most peers.

Valuation vs. Peers

At first glance, Arch Capital doesn't scream as cheap. Today, Arch trades at about 1.9 book value, near the upper end of its 10-year range. While this premium may appear high, it reflects the company's superior underwriting returns and low volatility of book value growth relative to peers like Chubb and Travelers.

Metric (2024E)Arch Capital (ACGL)Chubb (CB)Everest Group (EG)RenaissanceRe (RNR)Berkshire Hathaway (BRK)
Forward P/E~11x~12x~9x~8x~13x
Price-to-Book~2.0x~1.8x~1.2x~1.4x~1.5x
Return on Equity (ROE)~19%~14%~12%~13%~11%
Combined Ratio~83%~88%~91%~90%~93% (insurance segment)
Book Value CAGR (10yr)~12%~7%~6%~7%~9%

While Everest and Renaissance trade cheaper on book multiples, their underwriting results and ROE lag Arch by a wide margin. Chubb comes closer in scale and quality but hasn't matched Arch's efficiency or book value growth. Even Berkshire, with its unmatched capital base, trades cheaper on a book multiple but has a lower ROE and slower per-share book compounding. This leaves Arch in a rare position: priced above most peers on traditional multiples, but arguably still undervalued relative to its profitability and long-term compounding power. Investors who view book value growth as the truest measure of insurer performance will find Arch still offers a superior balance of quality and price.

Investors Activity

Arch Capital has steadily attracted interest from some of the most disciplined institutional managers, even as its premium valuation signals high expectations. This pattern reflects long-term conviction in the company's underwriting quality and capital allocation discipline.

Michael Steinberg's Steinberg Asset Management holds roughly 76,000 shares, a position it has maintained through several underwriting cycles. This isn't a high-turnover shop; it typically seeks capital-efficient franchises that can compound book value per share at above-market rates.

On the larger end, Egerton Capital UK, under John Armitage, owns more than 4.2 million shares (about $386 million in value). Armitage is known for favoring businesses with sustainable earnings quality rather than headline growth, and his steady build in Arch reflects confidence that its premium combined ratios and ROE are durable.

Millennium Management, run by Israel Englander, dramatically increased its position by 144% last quarter to nearly 1.84 million shares. Millennium's model-driven allocations typically react to improving risk-adjusted returns, suggesting a strong short-term statistical setup in addition to long-term appeal.

Smaller but notable holders include Jeff Auxier (Trades, Portfolio)'s Auxier Asset Management and Jeffries Financial Group, both of which initiated or increased stakes modestly, signaling a diversified base of value-focused and quantitative interest.

Even Dimensional Fund Advisors, known for its low-turnover, factor-based approach, holds more than 4 million shares, a quiet but powerful endorsement of Arch's fundamentals.

Taken together, these positions paint a picture of a stock favored not by momentum traders, but by patient, fundamentals-first investors who prize steady compounding over flashy growth. That shareholder base helps support Arch's ability to invest through cycles and maintain its disciplined underwriting culture.

Risks and Considerations

Despite its strong underwriting culture and enviable returns, Arch Capital is not immune to the realities of the insurance cycle. The company has built much of its growth on specialty lines and reinsurance, both of which can experience sharp swings when catastrophe losses spike or when competitive pricing erodes margins. Even a firm with Arch's discipline cannot fully escape soft markets; sustained premium declines or higher-than-expected claims could compress underwriting profitability. Another factor is valuation risk. At ~1.9 book value, Arch trades at a premium not only to peers like Chubb and Travelers, but even to broader insurers such as Markel. That multiple reflects confidence in Arch's superior ROE, but it leaves less margin of safety if underwriting results normalize. For investors, this means future compounding is tied not just to business performance but also to the market's continued willingness to pay up for it.

There's also exposure to macro and regulatory shifts. Rising interest rates have been a tailwind, boosting investment income, but a reversal in yields could weaken that contribution. Meanwhile, evolving global solvency standards may eventually require higher capital buffers, which would reduce flexibility for buybacks or reinvestment.

Finally, while Arch has grown through carefully targeted acquisitions, the company's reliance on reinsurance and specialty markets inherently ties it to large-loss events. A year of outsized catastrophe claims, hurricanes, wildfires, or earthquakes could test its risk management and temporarily dent book value growth.

Conclusion

Arch Capital has built one of the most enviable franchises in specialty insurance and reinsurance. Its decentralized underwriting, disciplined capital allocation, and consistent ability to deliver ROEs in the high teens put it in rare company. Few insurers have managed to grow book value at a near double-digit clip for two decades while maintaining conservative balance sheet strength and opportunistic share buybacks. Yet that very record of outperformance is reflected in the valuation. At nearly 1.9 book and ~14 forward earnings, Arch commands a premium over peers such as Chubb, Travelers, and even the broader specialty insurer cohort. For long-term investors, the key question is whether the company can sustain both its superior underwriting and its capital efficiency long enough to justify those multiples.

Patience remains rewarded in this story. Arch's resilience through cycles suggests it will continue to grow book value and reinvest at high returns, even if headline results face occasional setbacks from catastrophe years or competitive pricing pressure. For those willing to accept a higher entry valuation, the company offers one of the clearest examples of long-term discipline in an industry where many stumble. In a sector defined by volatility and capital missteps, Arch Capital stands out not because it avoids risk, but because it manages it better than most. That may not guarantee market-beating returns from today's premium, but it does position the company as one of the few insurers investors can trust to compound intrinsic value across cycles.

data.title: Shiba Inu Crashes Hard to New 2025 Low, Will SHIB Add Extra Zero?
data.description:
Shiba Inu crashed to a new low, reaching $0.0000085 on Friday, its lowest level in 2025 so far.

The drop coincided with a broader crypto market crash referred to as the "largest liquidation event in crypto history."

Crypto traders were hit by record liquidations, with volatility triggered in large part by macroeconomic concerns, although market weakness had already been present coming into Friday. Daily Chart, Courtesy: TradingView">

Over the past 24 hours, $19.36 billion has been wiped out, and over 1.6 million (1,666,610) traders liquidated, according to CoinGlass data.

CoinGlass in a tweet described the market sell-off as the "largest liquidation event in crypto history," adding that the actual total might likely be much higher as Binance only reports one liquidation order per second.

SHIB hits 2025 floor

Shiba Inu fell sharply to a low of $0.0000085 on Friday, from a high of $0.00001215, building on an earlier drop in the week. Shiba Inu has steadily declined from a high of $0.00001302 on Oct. 6, marking two out of three days in red since this date, with Friday marking the third day and the largest of the drops.

The positivity is that Shiba Inu bulls quickly swooped in to arrest the decline, with Shiba Inu sharply rebounding to erase the extra zero added, currently trading above $0.00001.

At the time of writing, Shiba Inu was still lower, down 11.86% in the last 24 hours to $0.00001087 and down 16% weekly.

Going forward, it will be watched to see if SHIB sustains above $0.00001 in order to target $0.0000125 and $0.000013.

Momentum indicators are hinting at oversold signals, suggesting Shiba Inu may face a relief rally or at least a dead cat bounce in the coming sessions.
